rows.title: CDMG
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[l_quotient_vs_ordered_pair_typing_inconsistent] The set $L$ of bidirected edges may be encoded either as a subset of the quotient $V \times V / {\sim}$ (with $(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)$, i.e., as unordered pairs) or as a subset of $V \times V$ subject to the symmetry constraint $(v_1, v_2) \in L \iff (v_2, v_1) \in L$. Both encodings are admissible; in the ordered-pair encoding, membership of $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(v_2, v_1)$ must always coincide. The irreflexivity condition $v_1 \neq v_2$ applies under either encoding.

[edge_set_disjointness_under_specified] The qualifier "disjoint" applied to the edge sets $E$ and $L$ is to be read as trivial type-level disjointness — directed edges and bidirected edges are objects of distinct types — and imposes no graph-theoretic mutual-exclusion constraint between them. In particular, for $v_1, v_2 \in V$, it is permitted to have $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ and $\{v_1, v_2\} \in L$ simultaneously; if a formalization encodes both $E$ and $L$ as subsets of ordered pairs over $V$, the same ordered pair $(v, w)$ may belong to both $E$ and $L$.

[manual_1] Node sets J and V are both finite.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_edges_quotient_vs_symmetry_redundancy
explanation: The bidirected edge set is declared as `L \ins V \times V/((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))`, i.e. a subset of the quotient of `V × V` by the swap relation. Elements of this quotient are unordered pairs (equivalence classes). But the accompanying condition is written as

    "$(v_1,v_2) \in L \, \implies\, v_1\neq v_2 \land (v_2,v_1) \in L$"

which treats elements of `L` as if they were ordered pairs.

If `L` really is a subset of the quotient, then `(v_1, v_2)` and `(v_2, v_1)` denote the *same* equivalence class, so "$(v_2, v_1) \in L$" follows automatically from "$(v_1, v_2) \in L$" — that half of the conjunction is vacuous. Only the `v_1 ≠ v_2` clause (no self-loops in `L`) carries content.

Alternatively, if the author actually intends `L \subseteq V \times V` (no quotient) with the implication forcing symmetry, then the quotient notation is misleading — it suggests a different ambient set.

This ambiguity matters for any encoding: should `L` be a `Sym2 V`, a `Finset (V × V)` with a symmetry axiom, or an irreflexive symmetric relation? The LN simultaneously suggests two of these.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: directed_self_loops_allowed_but_bidirected_self_loops_forbidden
explanation: The constraint `v_1 \neq v_2` is imposed on `L` (no bidirected self-loops), but `E` has no analogous constraint. The literal definition `E \ins (J \cup V) \times V` permits a pair `(v, v)` with `v \in V`, since then `v \in J \cup V` and `v \in V`. So a directed self-loop `v \to v` on an output node is admissible, while a bidirected self-loop `v \leftrightarrow v` is explicitly forbidden.

This asymmetry between E and L may be intentional (e.g. directed self-loops sometimes encode feedback) but the LN doesn't justify or even acknowledge it. A reader scanning the definition could easily assume self-loops are uniformly disallowed. Downstream notions like acyclicity (def_3_6) will need to take a stance on whether `v \to v` counts as a length-1 cycle.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: empty_cdmg_admitted_by_active_definition
explanation: The active text imposes no non-emptiness condition on `J \cup V`. Hence the literal definition admits the fully empty CDMG with `J = V = E = L = \emptyset`. This is a classic degenerate corner case: any downstream definition that implicitly assumes a non-empty vertex set (walks of positive length, "the source of a directed edge", topological orders on non-empty supports, etc.) needs to either gracefully handle, or explicitly exclude, the empty graph. Worth flagging because future claims/proofs that quantify "for every CDMG …" will quietly include this trivial case.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: CDMGNotation
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail] CDMGs do not admit a tail-tail edge type; consequently the placeholder rule "the star stands for arrowhead or tail" yields only the three enumerated configurations in item 7 (the tail-tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight). Any case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

[huh_visual_symmetry_vs_ordered_pair_in_L] The relation $L$ is required to be symmetric: for all $v_1, v_2$, $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ if and only if $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. Consequently, $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$, and the symmetry of the $\huh$-disjunct propagates to items 5–7 accordingly.

wording_check.output:
I've reviewed the tex block carefully and cross-checked the CDMG definition (def_3_1) and the usage of `\sus` in def_3_3 (as "adjacent").

```
SUBTLETY:
id: star_placeholder_omits_tail_tail_in_sus
explanation: The trailing sentence says "The star stands for a placeholder to mean: ``arrowhead or tail''.''
Read literally, this is a meta-rule: any star in $\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$ can be expanded to either "arrowhead" (i.e.\ head) or "tail". Mechanically applying that rule to $\sus$ gives four combinations for (left, right):

  (tail, tail) — $\tut$
  (tail, head) — $\tuh$
  (head, tail) — $\hut$
  (head, head) — $\huh$

But the actual enumeration in the definition lists only three of these:

  ``$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$''

The fourth case, $\tut$ (tail--tail), is silently omitted. The omission is consistent with the CDMG edge inventory of def_3_1 — directed edges in $E$ have one tail and one head, bidirected edges in $L$ have two heads, and no edge type has two tails — so $\tut$ is vacuous as an edge of $G$ regardless. So the enumeration is semantically correct *for CDMG*.

However, the literal text creates a mild internal inconsistency: the meta-description ("star = arrowhead or tail") predicts 4 cases for $\sus$, while the enumeration gives 3. A reader (or a formalizer mechanically unfolding the placeholder) would expect either (a) the meta-rule to qualify itself with "...such that the resulting edge type exists in the graph", or (b) an explicit tail--tail clause that resolves to $\bot$.

A concrete corner case: a Lean encoding that defines $\mathrm{star}$ as an inductive with two constructors {arrowhead, tail} and then defines $\sus$ by pattern-matching all $2\times 2 = 4$ combinations would faithfully follow the meta-rule but would need a fourth (vacuous) case the LN doesn't spell out. Conversely, an encoding that hard-codes only the three listed cases is faithful to the enumeration but not to the meta-description.

Downstream, def_3_3 uses $\sus$ to define adjacency. The 3-case enumeration matches the intended "any edge between $v_1$ and $v_2$" meaning, so the substantive content is fine; the subtlety is purely in the wording of the placeholder explanation versus the explicit list.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: EdgeRelations
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of] Throughout the enumeration above, the writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the same edge (and the bidirected writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ likewise coincide). Membership of an edge in the categories "into $v$" or "out of $v$" depends only on the underlying element of $E$, not on which of these textual writings is used; item 3's explicit listing of both writings and item 2's single-writing enumeration are therefore equivalent under this identification.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: out_of_v2_not_literally_defined
explanation: Item 2 separately defines "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" using a fixed edge ordering ($v_1 \star v_2$) and gives two distinct cases. Item 3, by contrast, only defines "out of $v_1$" and absorbs the two orderings of writing the edge into a single case: "$v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$". The literal text never defines "out of $v_2$" at all. By analogy a reader would expect "out of $v_2$" to be: edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_2 \tuh v_1$ — but that has to be supplied by the reader; it is not in the tex. A pedantic formalizer will encode "out of $v_1$" exactly as written and find that downstream claims that range over arbitrary vertices ("for every $v \in V$, the edges out of $v$ …") strictly speaking only cover the case where $v$ has been bound to the first label of the edge.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_huh_into_both_out_of_neither
explanation: Under the literal reading of item 2, the bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ is "into $v_1$" AND "into $v_2$". Under item 3, no $\huh$-edge ever counts as "out of $v_1$" — the enumerated forms are only $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$. So a $\huh$-edge is "into both endpoints" but "out of neither". This is consistent with CDMG semantics (a bidirected edge denotes latent confounding, with no directed influence either way), but it has a counter-intuitive consequence: "into" and "out of" are NOT complementary classifications of incident edges. A reader or formalizer who treats "out of $v$" as shorthand for "incident at $v$ but not into $v$" will get the wrong answer for $\huh$-edges — they are "into" and "not out of". This matters for any downstream notion built on "out of" (out-degree, children, directed-out-neighbourhood), where bidirected edges must contribute zero, not "the remainder after removing in-edges". Corner case: if a vertex $v$ has only $\huh$-edges incident to it, then $v$ has no out-edges at all, despite having non-zero adjacency.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: CDMGRestrictions
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause] The statements "$j \hus v \notin G$" and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" carry implicit universal quantification over $v$. Precisely: for every $j \in J$ and every node $v$ in the graph, $j \hus v \notin G$; and for every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, an edge $j \tuh v$ is permitted in $G$ (its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it).

wording_check.output:
I now have full context. Let me audit each statement of the remark carefully against the CDMG definition and notation.

**Statement 1**: "$j \hus v \notin G$" — given $\hus = \hut$ or $\huh$. For $j \hut v$: needs $(v,j) \in E \ins (J\cup V) \times V$, forcing $j \in V$, contradicts $j \in J$. For $j \huh v$: needs $(j,v) \in L \ins V \times V$, again forces $j \in V$. ✓

**Statement 2**: "$j \tuh v$ are allowed" with $v \in V$ — directly matches $E$'s codomain constraint. ✓

**Statement 3**: "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" — follows from edge-type constraints (no $j_1 \tuh j_2$ since target must be in $V$; no $j_1 \huh j_2$ since both ends must be in $V$). ✓

Corner cases ($J = \emptyset$, $V = \emptyset$, singleton $J$) all check out vacuously.

The variable $v$ in statement 1 is implicitly universally quantified — standard math-text convention (and the not-cdmg bullets confirm $v$ defaults to ranging over $G = J \cup V$). The mild inconsistency where statement 2 re-binds $v$ to $V$ explicitly does not create a substantive ambiguity since both readings yield equivalent truth values (the J-J cases are excluded by the edge-set definitions regardless). The "Furthermore" introduces a consequence, not new content — but the remark itself is a derived characterization, so that's expected.

NO_SUBTLETIES

rows.title: Walks
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Walks]\label{def:walks}
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $v,w \in G$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item A \emph{walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a finite alternating sequence of adjacent nodes and edges 
      \[v=v_0, a_0,  v_1, \dots v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n=w\] 
%      \[v=v_0 \sus v_1 \sus  \cdots \sus v_{n-1} \sus v_n=w\]
        in $G$ for some $n \ge 0$, i.e.\ such that for every $k=0,\dots,n-1$ 
        we have that $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \cup L$ or $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$, and with end nodes $v_0=v$ and $v_n=w$.
        An example walk from $v_0$ to $v_3$ could look like:
        \[v_0 \tuh v_1 \hut  v_2 \huh v_3, \qquad\text{ with } \qquad  v_0 \tuh v_1, v_2 \tuh v_1 \in E,\,v_2 \huh v_3 \in L. \]
        The same node may appear multiple times in a walk.
        Also the \emph{trivial walk} consisting of a single node $v_0 \in G$ is allowed (if $v=w$).
        The walk is called \emph{into $v_0$} if $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$, and \emph{out of $v_0$} if $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$.
        Similarly, it is called \emph{into $v_n$} if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$ and \emph{out of $v_n$} if $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$.
    \item A \emph{directed walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh  \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$,
        where all arrowheads point in the direction of $w$ and there are no arrowheads pointing back.
   % \item[] Directed walks exclude the trivial walk per definition.
    \item A \emph{bidirected walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \huh v_1 \huh  \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$, where all edges are bidirected.
    \item A \emph{collider walk} from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh  \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w,\]
        for some $n \ge 0$, where all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them (a.k.a.\ collider).
        Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$.
    \item A walk is called \emph{path} if no node occurs more than once.
    \item A \emph{bifurcation} between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk of the form:
        \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut  \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k  \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
   %             \[v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut  \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \huh v_k  \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w,\]
        such that $v \ne w$, the walk contains both endnodes exactly once, 
	the subwalk $v_0 \hut \cdots v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$,
	and the subwalk $v_k \tuh \cdots v_n$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$.
        %every node has at most one arrowhead pointing towards it, and 
        %both endnodes have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. 
        If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has \emph{source} $v_{k}$.
  \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_right_chain_trivial_is_just_directed_walk] As an additional constraint on the bifurcation form, both endnodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ must have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. In particular, this excludes the degenerate case $k = n$ with directed hinge $v_{n-1} \hut v_n$, in which the walk reduces to a directed walk $v_0 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow v_n$ and $v_n$ has no arrowhead pointing towards it.

[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note] For $n=1$, a collider walk from $v$ to $w$ requires a bidirected edge $v \huh w \in E$ (arrowheads at both endpoints), as dictated by the form $v_0 \suh v_1$ combined with $v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ collapsing onto the single edge. The inline note "$v \sus w \in G$" is to be read in this stricter sense and does *not* admit purely directed edges $v \tuh w$ or $v \hut w$ as collider walks of length 1.

wording_check.output:
Looking at the LN text for def_3_4 carefully, I'll examine each item for subtleties.

The walk types admit `n ≥ 0` (so trivial walks count). "Into/out of" is defined symbolically using $\hus$, $\tuh$, etc. The collider walk has an explicit $n=1$ clarification. The bifurcation pattern allows wide ranges of $k$ and $n$.

Let me identify several genuine issues:

```
SUBTLETY:
id: collider_walk_n1_literal_pattern_vs_note
explanation: The collider-walk pattern
  "v=v_0 \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n=w"
for n=1 literally has v_{n-1}=v_0 and v_n=v_1, so the single edge between v_0 and v_1 must satisfy *both* the "first-slot" constraint $v_0 \suh v_1$ (any-tail at v_0, arrowhead at v_1) and the "last-slot" constraint $v_{n-1} \hus v_n = v_0 \hus v_1$ (arrowhead at v_0, any at v_1). Combined, this forces the lone edge to be bidirected ($v_0 \huh v_1$).
However, the LN explicitly states: "Note that for $n=1$ this reads: $v\sus w \in G$" — i.e., any edge between v and w qualifies.
So the symbolic pattern, read literally, is strictly more restrictive than the author's stated meaning. The author is silently relying on a "the $\suh$/$\hus$ marks only constrain the side facing interior colliders" reading, which is also consistent with the verbal description "all nodes in between $v$ and $w$ have two arrowheads pointing towards them" (for n=1 there are no in-between nodes, so no constraint). A naive formalizer who follows the literal pattern would reject e.g. $v_0 \tuh v_1$ as a length-1 collider walk; the author's note says it should be accepted. Downstream, this matters wherever "collider walk of length 1 from v to w" is used as a synonym for "v and w are adjacent in any edge type".
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_admits_n1_k1_single_backward_or_bidirected_edge
explanation: The bifurcation pattern
  "v=v_0 \hut v_1 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n=w"
imposes only $1 \le k \le n$ (so that $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ exist), $v \ne w$, "the walk contains both endnodes exactly once", and that the two subwalks are directed walks. The directed-walk subclass admits $n \ge 0$, so the *trivial* subwalk consisting of a single node *is* a directed walk.

Consequence: for n=1, k=1, the left subwalk is just the node $v_0$ (trivial directed walk from $v_0$ to $v_0$), the right subwalk is just $v_1$ (trivial directed walk from $v_1$ to $v_1$), and the entire bifurcation collapses to the single edge $v_0 \hus v_1$. The $\hus$ admits:
  - $v_0 \hut v_1$, i.e. the directed edge $w \to v$ ("backward" relative to the bifurcation's stated direction "between $v$ and $w$");
  - $v_0 \huh v_1$, i.e. a single bidirected edge.

Neither of these visually "bifurcates" — there is no node from which two distinct directed walks emanate. A single arrow $w \to v$ is literally called a "bifurcation between $v$ and $w$" by the definition, which seems to contradict the intuitive meaning of the word. The same degeneracy occurs more generally whenever $k=1$ alone or $k=n$ alone: one of the two arms is empty, so the structure is just a directed walk plus a backward/bidirected edge at one end, not a true Y-shape.

Downstream claims that rely on "a bifurcation has two distinct directed walks emanating from a common node" would fail for these degenerate instances unless the definition is tightened (e.g. requiring $1 < k < n$, or equivalently that each subwalk be non-trivial).
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_source_can_be_endnode
explanation: The final sentence says: "If the edge $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ is directed ($v_{k-1} \hut v_k$) then we say that the bifurcation has source $v_k$."
The "source" is intuitively the apex of the Y-shape — a node strictly between the two endpoints with outgoing arrows toward both. But the literal definition assigns "source $= v_k$" whenever the middle edge is directed, regardless of whether $v_k$ is an internal node or an endpoint.

Concrete corner case: when $k=n$, $v_k = v_n = w$ — so the source of the bifurcation is the endnode $w$ itself. For example, $n=2, k=2$ gives the walk $v_0 \hut v_1 \hut v_2$ (i.e. $v_2 \to v_1 \to v_0$), and the LN calls this a "bifurcation between $v_0$ and $v_2$ with source $v_2$" — but this is literally just a directed walk from $v_2$ to $v_0$ with no branching. The endpoint $w=v_2$ is declared the "source" even though it has only one outgoing arrow (toward $v_1$), not two.

Similarly, when $n=k=1$ with a directed middle edge $v_0 \hut v_1$ (i.e. $w \to v$), the source is again the endnode $w$.

This makes "source of a bifurcation" potentially equal to one of the named endnodes $v$ or $w$, which is surprising terminology and could cause confusion in downstream proofs that treat the source as a distinguished interior node.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: into_out_of_undefined_for_trivial_walk
explanation: The "into/out of" classifications are defined via $a_0$ and $a_{n-1}$:
  - "into $v_0$" iff $a_0 = v_0 \hus v_1$
  - "out of $v_0$" iff $a_0 = v_0 \tuh v_1$
  - "into $v_n$" iff $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \suh v_n$
  - "out of $v_n$" iff $a_{n-1} = v_{n-1} \hut v_n$

For the trivial walk ($n=0$, single node $v_0=v=w$), neither $a_0$ nor $a_{n-1}$ exists, so the walk is *neither* into nor out of its (single) endpoint, on either side. The LN does not explicitly address this; it's vacuous by the symbolic definitions.

This is fine in isolation, but downstream lemmas that treat "into/out of" as a dichotomy (e.g. claiming "every walk from $v$ to $w$ is either into $v_0$ or out of $v_0$") would break for the trivial walk. A formalizer should be aware that these four predicates can all be simultaneously false on a walk.

Also worth noting: for bidirected first edges ($a_0 = v_0 \huh v_1$), the walk is "into $v_0$" (because $\hus$ matches $\huh$) but NOT "out of $v_0$" (because $\tuh$ requires a tail at $v_0$). So "into" and "out of" are mutually exclusive but not exhaustive even for non-trivial walks — bidirected ends are "into" only.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: FamilyRelationships
rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Family relationships]
     Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $v,w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
     We then define:
     \begin{enumerate}
         \item The set of \emph{parents} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, w \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v).\]
         %\item[] \PF{maybe better for sets (???) :The set of \emph{parents} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Pa^G(A):= \lp \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)\rp \sm A.\]
         %    but then  if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\lC v\rC ) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$ ... }
         \item The set of \emph{children} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
         \item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{siblings} of $v$ in $G$: \[\Sib^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\, v \huh w \in G \rC.\]
         %\item[] The set of \emph{children} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Ch^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Ch^G(v).\]
         \item The set of \emph{ancestors} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Anc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Anc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{ancestors} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Anc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Anc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Anc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{descendants} of $v$ in $G$: 
             \[\Desc^G(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ directed walk: } v \tuh \cdots \tuh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v \in \Desc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The set of \emph{descendants} of $A$ in $G$: \[\Desc^G(A):= \bigcup_{v \in A} \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Desc^G(A)$.
         \item The set of \emph{non-descendants} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A).\]
         \item The \emph{strongly connected component} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(v) := \Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v).\]
             Note: $v \in \Sc^G(v)$.
         \item[] The (union of) \emph{strongly connected components} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[ \Sc^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Sc^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Sc^G(A)$.
         \item The \emph{district} of $v$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(v) := \lC w \in G\,|\, \exists \text{ bidirected walk: } 
             v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w \in G \rC.\]
             Note: $v\in \Dist^G(v)$.
         \item[] The \emph{district} of $A$ in $G$:
             \[\Dist^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Dist^G(v).\]
             Note: $A \ins \Dist^G(A)$.
         %\item The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $v$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(v):=\lC w \in G\,|\,\exists \text{ collider walk: } 
         %    v \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus w \in G \rC \sm \{v\}.\]
         %    Note: $\lp\Pa^G(v) \cup \Ch^G(v) \cup \Pa^G(\Ch^G(v))\rp \sm \{v\} \ins \MBl^G_d(v)$.
         %\item[] The d-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_d(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \MBl^G_d(v) \sm A.\]
         %\item The $\sigma$-\emph{Markov blanket} of $A$ in $G$:
         %    \[\MBl^G_\sigma(A):= \MBl^{G^\acy}_d(A), \]
         %    where $G^\acy$ is an acyclification of $G$, a notion defined below in Definition~\ref{def:cdg_acyclification}.
     \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[self_membership_notes_require_length_zero_walks] In the definitions of $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$, the directed and bidirected walks are understood to include the trivial walk of length $0$ consisting of the single vertex $v$ with no edges. Consequently, $v \in \Anc^G(v)$, $v \in \Desc^G(v)$, $v \in \Sc^G(v)$, and $v \in \Dist^G(v)$ hold for every $v \in V$ unconditionally, regardless of whether any edge (in particular any self-loop) incident to $v$ exists in $G$.

[type_mismatch_individual_vs_set_versions] The preamble should read "$v, w \in J \cup V$" rather than "$v, w \in V$": the individual definitions $\Pa^G(v)$, $\Ch^G(v)$, $\Sib^G(v)$, $\Anc^G(v)$, $\Desc^G(v)$, $\Sc^G(v)$, and $\Dist^G(v)$ are defined for any $v \in J \cup V$, using the same set-builder expressions given. In particular, input nodes ($v \in J$) may have children, descendants, etc., and the set-version definitions $\Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)$ (and analogously for the other operators) are well-typed for $A \ins J \cup V$ because each summand is defined.

[district_walk_indexing_ambiguous_for_small_n] $w \in \Dist^G(v)$ iff there exists a walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ in which every edge is bidirected ($\huh$). The walk may have any length $\geq 0$; in particular, the length-$0$ walk at $v$ witnesses $v \in \Dist^G(v)$, so neither a bidirected self-loop at $v$ nor a length-$2$ bidirected cycle through $v$ is required. The intermediate-node indexing $v_1, \ldots, v_{n-1}$ in the displayed formula should be read as syntactic sugar for "walk consisting of bidirected edges" and imposes no lower bound on walk length.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: trivial_walk_implicit_in_self_membership_notes
explanation: The notes "$v \in \Anc^G(v)$", "$v \in \Desc^G(v)$", and "$v \in \Dist^G(v)$" require that the trivial (length-0) walk from a node to itself counts as a "directed walk" (resp. "bidirected walk"). But the literal pictorial body of each definition suggests at least one edge: "$\exists \text{ directed walk: } w \tuh \cdots \tuh v$" and "$\exists \text{ bidirected walk: } v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w$". A literal reading of the formula alone (with no edge-free convention) would force $\Anc^G(v)$ to be empty when $v$ has no incoming directed self-loop, contradicting the note. So either def_3_4 (Walks) admits length-0 walks by convention, or the note is implicitly adding a "$\cup \{v\}$" — these are different mathematical objects and a formalizer must commit to one. Downstream: any later claim relying on $v \in \Anc^G(v)$ (e.g., reflexivity of the ancestor relation, or any well-founded induction on ancestor depth) is sensitive to which reading is canonical.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: self_loop_makes_v_its_own_parent_child_sibling
explanation: The literal definitions
\[\Pa^G(v):=\{w \in G \mid w \tuh v \in G\},\quad \Ch^G(v):=\{w \in G \mid v \tuh w \in G\},\quad \Sib^G(v):=\{w \in G \mid v \huh w \in G\}\]
all admit the case $w = v$ when a self-loop $v \tuh v$ (or $v \huh v$) is present in $G$. The author is explicitly aware of this for parents — the commented-out alternative reads "if $v \tuh v \in E$ then $v \notin \Pa^G(\{v\}) \neq \Pa^G(v) \ni v$", and the chosen definition deliberately keeps $v \in \Pa^G(v)$ to preserve $\Pa^G(\{v\}) = \Pa^G(v)$. So for parents the convention is intentional, but the same corner case for $\Ch^G$ and $\Sib^G$ is not flagged. A formalizer needs to know self-loops yield $v \in \Pa^G(v) \cap \Ch^G(v) \cap \Sib^G(v)$ — this is the chosen reading, not an oversight. Downstream: any claim of the form "$\Pa^G(v) \cap \{v\} = \emptyset$" or "siblings are distinct from $v$" will fail in CDMGs that allow self-loops.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: domain_mismatch_between_setup_and_set_versions
explanation: The setup declares "$v, w \in V$ and $A \ins J \cup V$". The per-node definitions then read e.g. "$\Pa^G(v) := \{w \in G \mid w \tuh v \in G\}$" — stated for $v \in V$. But the set version "$\Pa^G(A) := \bigcup_{v \in A} \Pa^G(v)$" ranges $v$ over $A \subseteq J \cup V$, so the inner $\Pa^G(v)$ is being invoked with $v$ possibly a context node in $J$ — outside the stated domain of the per-node definition. The formula still makes literal sense (the set-builder notation works for any node), but the variable declaration is too narrow to type-check the set unfolding without an implicit extension. A formalizer must decide: do parents/children/siblings/ancestors/etc. take inputs in $V$, or in $J \cup V$? In CDMGs context nodes typically have no incoming arrows, so $\Pa^G(j) = \emptyset$ for $j \in J$ is the harmless default, but this is not made explicit.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: district_walk_indexing_ambiguous_for_small_n
explanation: The district definition writes the witnessing walk as "$v \huh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \huh w$". For $n = 1$ the indices $v_1, \ldots, v_0$ are empty, leaving "$v \huh w$" (a single bidirected edge); for $n = 0$ they are also empty, but now both literal endpoints written are $v$ and $w$ with no edges between them, which can only mean $v = w$ (trivial walk). The text never states what $n$ counts (edges? intermediate vertices? vertices?), nor what the smallest legal $n$ is. Combined with the note "$v \in \Dist^G(v)$" (which seems to require $n = 0$ to be allowed and to mean a trivial walk), this leaves the formalizer to commit to a convention that the LN never states. The same indexing concern does not appear for $\Anc^G/\Desc^G$, where the body uses "$\cdots$" without bound variables, but the reader is still forced to fill in "walks include length 0".
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: nondesc_only_defined_for_sets
explanation: $\NonDesc^G$ is defined only for a subset $A$: "$\NonDesc^G(A) := (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(A)$". Every other family relation in this definition (Pa, Ch, Sib, Anc, Desc, Sc, Dist) has both a single-vertex form and a set form, with the set form built from the vertex form by union. The asymmetry is mild — $\NonDesc^G(v)$ can be read as $\NonDesc^G(\{v\}) = (J \cup V) \sm \Desc^G(v)$ — but it is not spelled out, and a future claim phrased as "$\NonDesc^G(v)$" will need a reader to silently insert the singleton coercion. Note also that "non-descendants" via this definition is NOT the complement of $\Desc$ in $V$ alone but in $J \cup V$, so context nodes are always non-descendants of any non-empty $A$ (unless they happen to be descendants), which is a deliberate but not advertised choice.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: Acyclicity
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [nontrivial_directed_walk_not_defined_in_block] A non-trivial directed walk is a directed walk $v_0 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ with $n \ge 1$ (i.e., one that traverses at least one edge); the trivial walk consisting of a single node $v_0$ is excluded. In particular, a self-loop $v \tuh v$ constitutes a non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself, so an acyclic CDMG contains no self-loops on any $v \in V$.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: non_trivial_walk_undefined_admits_self_loop_and_2cycle_ambiguity
explanation: The block uses "non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself" without defining "non-trivial" anywhere in this definition. Two plausible readings:

  (a) **Length ≥ 1**: any closed walk other than the zero-length walk at $v$. Under this reading, a self-loop $v \to v$ is itself a non-trivial walk from $v$ to $v$, so acyclicity forbids self-loops, and a 2-cycle $v \to w \to v$ is also forbidden.

  (b) **Length ≥ 2** (or "passes through a vertex other than $v$"): self-loops would not count as non-trivial, so a CDMG consisting of just $v \to v$ would still be "acyclic" under this reading. Some authors use "non-trivial" this way when they want to single out "real" cycles.

  The standard graph-theoretic intent is almost certainly (a), but the LN never says so. This becomes a real corner-case question whenever the rest of the LN reasons about acyclic CDMGs: for instance, any later argument that invokes a topological ordering of $V$ implicitly needs self-loops excluded, i.e. reading (a). Conversely, any later claim that an acyclic CDMG may have $v \to v$ as a benign self-edge would force reading (b). Without an explicit anchor, downstream proofs can silently rely on whichever convention the formalizer chose.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: node_quantifier_scope_v_in_G_unclear_J_versus_V
explanation: The quantifier reads "for any node $v \in G$", but $G = (J, V, E, L)$ is a 4-tuple, not a set of nodes. The literal text leaves it unclear whether $v$ ranges over

  (i) just $V$, or
  (ii) $V \cup J$ (the full node set, assuming $J$ also consists of nodes).

  In a CDMG, $J$ typically plays a distinguished role (e.g., context/parameter/index nodes that do not receive directed edges, or that are treated structurally differently from $V$). If acyclicity is meant to be a condition only on the endogenous part $V$, the definition should say "$v \in V$"; if it's meant to apply to all nodes regardless of role, it should say "$v \in V \cup J$" (and even then $v \in G$ is sloppy).

  Corner case: suppose a node $j \in J$ has only outgoing edges (by construction of CDMG). Then no walk from $j$ to $j$ exists anyway, so under reading (ii) the condition is trivially satisfied at $j$ — no practical difference. But if the CDMG model permits some incoming structure at $J$, the two readings genuinely diverge: a $j$-to-$j$ walk could exist and would only be forbidden under reading (ii). A formalizer must pick one; the LN does not say which.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: TopologicalOrder

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: equivalent_indexing_assumes_finite_node_set
explanation: The LN gives a primary definition — "a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$ such that for all $v,w \in G$: $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$" — and then claims "Equivalently, it can be described as an indexing of the nodes $J \cup V = \{v_1,\dots,v_K\}$ where parents always precede their children." The two formulations are equivalent only when $J \cup V$ is finite (so that the strict total order has order type a finite ordinal and can be written as $v_1 < v_2 < \dots < v_K$). For an infinite node set, the primary definition is still meaningful (any total order on an infinite set is fine), but the "indexing" reformulation either fails entirely (uncountable case) or requires further conditions (the order type must be $\omega$ for a countable set). The definition does not state a finiteness hypothesis on $J \cup V$ — if CDMGs are taken to be finite implicitly (likely the intended convention), the equivalence is fine; if not, the "Equivalently" clause is genuinely stronger than the primary definition. A downstream argument that picks an index `i` and inducts on the index relies on the indexing formulation and would silently inherit a finiteness assumption.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: quantifier_domain_v_w_in_G_is_tuple_not_set
explanation: The implication is stated "for all $v,w \in G$", but $G = (J,V,E,L)$ is literally a 4-tuple, not a set, so "$v \in G$" does not type-check on the nose. The clearly intended domain is $J \cup V$ (matching "a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$" in the previous clause, and matching what makes "$v < w$" defined). This is conventional graph-theoretic shorthand and unambiguous in intent, but a formalization must commit to the domain explicitly — and in particular must ensure that whenever $v \in \Pa^G(w)$, $v$ is in fact in $J \cup V$ so that the comparison $v < w$ is defined. (For a CDMG this should follow from the definition of $\Pa^G$, but it is a side-condition the LN never spells out.)
END_SUBTLETY

rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Node-splitting on CDMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{node-split graph} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CDMG: 
    \[ G_{\spl(W)} :=\lp J_{\spl(W)}, V_{\spl(W)}, E_{\spl(W)},L_{\spl(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\spl(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^0:=\lC w^0\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^1:=\lC w^1 \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^0 \neq w^1$ for $w \in W$.
    Additionally (for convenience), for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^0:=v^1:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\spl(W)} := J$,
      \item $V_{\spl(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^0$.
      \item $E_{\spl(W)} := \lC v^1_1 \tuh v_2^0 \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \st w \in W \rC$,
      \item $L_{\spl(W)} :=\lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  So all incoming edges onto nodes in $W$ become incoming edges into the corresponding nodes in $W^0$,
  all outgoing edges out of nodes in $W$ become outgoing edges out of the corresponding nodes in $W^1$,
  and edges $w^0 \tuh w^1$ are added for all nodes in $W$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [disjointness_of_new_copies_only_partially_stipulated] The disjointness assertions $W^0 \cap V = W^1 \cap V = W^0 \cap J = W^1 \cap J = W^0 \cap W^1 = \emptyset$ are to be realized at the type level: in a formal encoding, $W^0$ and $W^1$ are constructed as tagged copies (e.g. via a $\Sigma$-type or tagged sum) so that their elements are of a distinct type from elements of $V$ and $J$, making disjointness hold automatically rather than as a side condition. The convention $v^0 := v^1 := v$ for $v \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ is purely notational shorthand used inside the definitions of $E_{\spl(W)}$ and $L_{\spl(W)}$, and does not reassign these elements to the tagged copy types.

\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Node-splitting hard intervention on CADMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{single-world intervention graph (SWIG)} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG: 
    \[ G_{\swig(W)} :=\lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)},L_{\swig(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\swig(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^o:=\lC w^o\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i:=\lC w^i \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$.
  However, for brevity, for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^o:=v^i:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i$,
      \item $V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^o$.
      \item $E_{\swig(W)} := \lC v^i_1 \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E  \rC$,
      \item $L_{\swig(W)} :=\lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$,
  and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}

\begin{Def}[Extending CDMGs with intervention nodes]\label{def:cdmg_intervention_nodes}
  Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes.
  The \emph{extended CDMG} of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W \ins J \cup V$  and corresponding \emph{intervention nodes} 
%  $I_W = \{I_w \,|\, w \in W\}$ \Joris{with $I_j:=j$ for $j \in J$},

  is the CDMG: 
  \[ G_{\doit(I_W)}:=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]
  %\[G(V|\doit(J \dcup I_W)):=G_{\doit(I_W)}=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]
  where:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]

      \item $J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \sm J \rC$, %not W \sm J, makes weak and hard interventions commute

rows.tex_block:
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{Rem}
    If a CDMG $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.
    So, the partial condition for $i\sigma$-separation
    ``a blockable non-collider in $C$''
    can be simplified to ``(any) non-collider in $C$''.
    
    So in the acyclic case we can simplify the notion of $i\sigma$-separation, 
    which we will refer to as \emph{$id$-separation}.
    However, in the non-acyclic setting $id$-separation (``(any) non-collider in $C$'') and $i\sigma$-separation (``a blockable non-collider in $C$'') are clearly not equivalent.

    It turned out that in the non-acyclic case $i\sigma$-separation is the more general concept (and as said above it also captures the acyclic case equivalently well), see \cite{FM17,FM18,FM20,BFPM21}.
    We will first focus on CADMGs (acyclic) for which we can restrict ourselves to the somewhat simpler $id$-separation.
    Later, we will pick up $i\sigma$-separation again when we deal with cycles.
\end{Rem}
\end{claimmark}

rows.tex_block:
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{Lem}\label{lem:replace_walk}
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $C \subseteq V \cup J$ and $\pi = \lp  v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp$ be a $C$-$\sigma$-open walk in $G$.
  Suppose $v_i \in \Sc^G(v_j)$ for some $i,j \in \{0,\dots,n\}$ with $i < j$.
If we then replace the subwalk $v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j$ of $\pi$ by 
  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
      \item a shortest directed path $v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_j$ in $G$ if $j = n$ or if $v_j \tuh v_{j+1}$ on $\pi$, or 
    \item a shortest directed path $v_i \hut \cdots \hut v_j$ in $G$ otherwise,
  \end{enumerate}
  then this new subwalk is entirely within $\Sc^G(v_j)$ and the modified walk $\pi'$ is still $C$-$\sigma$-open.
\begin{proof}
$\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at one of the initial nodes $v_0, \dots, v_{i-1}$ or at one of the final nodes $v_{j+1}, \dots, v_n$ on $\pi'$, since these nodes occur in the same local configuration on $\pi$ and are not $C$-$\sigma$-blocked on $\pi$ by assumption.
Furthermore, $\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at one of the nodes strictly between $v_i$ and $v_j$ on $\pi'$ (if there are any), since these nodes are all non-endnode non-colliders that only point to nodes in the same strongly connected component $\Sc^G(v_j)$. It is also worth noting that $\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at any of its endnodes, which could be $v_i$ or $v_j$ or both, because those are the same in $\pi$. So in the following we can w.l.o.g.\ assume that both $v_i$ and $v_j$ are non-endnodes of $\pi$ and thus $\pi'$.

Case (i). By assumption $v_j$ is either a fork or a right chain (or the right endnode) on $\pi$ that is $C$-$\sigma$-open. Since the same blocking criteria apply to $v_j$ on $\pi'$ it remains $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$. If $v_i=v_j$ then also $v_i$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$ (if $v_i$ is the left endnode or not). If $v_i \neq v_j$, then the new directed path $v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_j$ in $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open at $v_i$ because all nodes in between lie in the same stronly connected component $\Sc^G(v_i)$ (or $v_i$ is the left endnode anyways).

 Case (ii). Since case (i) is solved we can assume that we have $j<n$ with $v_j \hus v_{j+1}$ in $\pi$. 
 If $v_{i-1} \hut v_i$ on $\pi'$ (or $v_i$ the left endnode) then this case is analogous to case (i). 
 So we can also assume that we have $i > 0$ and $v_{i-1} \suh v_i$ on $\pi$. So $\pi$ looks as follows:
 \[\pi:\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots. \]
 So there must be a smallest number $k \in \lC i,\dots,j\rC$ such that a collider appears at $v_k$ on $\pi$:
 \[\pi:\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k \hus \cdots \sus v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots. \]
 Since $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open we have $v_k \in \Anc^G(C)$. Since $v_i \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ (otherwise $v_k$ would not be the first collider appearing after $v_i$) we thus have that also $v_i \in \Anc^G(C)$.
 So if we replace the subwalk $v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j$ of $\pi$ by the shortest directed path $v_i \hut \cdots \hut v_j$ in $G$ we then get for $\pi'$ the following situation:
 \[\pi':\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \hut \cdots \hut  v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots, \]
 which is then $C$-$\sigma$-open at $v_i$ as $v_i \in \Anc^G(C)$. Note that this holds also when $v_i=v_j$.
 If $v_i \neq v_j$ then $v_j$ is also $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$ as $v_j$ points left to a node in the same strongly connected component as $v_j$. 

 So in all cases $\pi'$ stays $C$-$\sigma$-open.
\end{proof}
\end{Lem}
\end{claimmark}

\begin{defmark}
\begin{Eg}[The standard acyclification]
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. Then we define the \emph{standard acyclification} of $G$ as the CDMG
    $ G'=(J,V,E',L')$
    where:
      \begin{align*} 
          E' := & \lC v_1 \tuh v_2  \st v_1 \in J \cup V, v_2 \in V, v_2 \notin \Sc^G(v_1),\exists v_2' \in \Sc^G(v_2):\, 
   v_1 \tuh v_2' \in E\rC,\\
          L' := &\lC v_1 \huh v_2  \st v_1,v_2 \in V, v_1 \neq v_2, \exists v_1' \in \Sc^G(v_1), v_2' \in \Sc^G(v_2):\, v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC \\ \cup &
\lC v_1 \huh v_2  \st v_1,v_2 \in V, v_1 \neq v_2, v_1 \in \Sc^G(v_2) \rC.
\end{align*}
% claim_3_32
\begin{claimmark}
The standard acyclification of a CDMG is acyclic, i.e.\ a CADMG.
\end{claimmark}
\begin{proof}
    Assume that $G'$ is not acyclic. Then there exists a non-trivial cyclic directed walk in $G'$:
    \[ v_1 \tuh v_2 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k \tuh v_1, \]
    for some $k \ge 1$. It is clear that $k \ge 2$ because clearly $v_1 \in \Sc^G(v_1)$, which rules out the existence of an edge $v_1 \tuh v_1 \in G'$. 
    For simplicity we now identify $v_{k+1} := v_1$ in the following.
    By construction of $G'$ for every $i=1,\dots,k$ there exists $v'_{i+1} \in \Sc^G(v_{i+1})$ 
    such that the edge $v_i \tuh v_{i+1}'$ exists in $G$. Since $v'_{i+1} \in \Sc^G(v_{i+1})$ there also exists a directed walk in $G$: 
     \[v'_{i+1} \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{i+1}.\]
     Concatenating all directed walks we get the cyclic directed walk in $G$:
    \[ v_1 \tuh v_2' \tuh \cdots \tuh v_2 \tuh v_3' \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k 
    \tuh v_1' \tuh \cdots \tuh v_1. \]
    This shows that $v_2 \in \Sc^G(v_1)$, which is a contradiction to the existence of the edge $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G'$.
    So a non-trivial cyclic directed walk in $G'$ cannot exist in the first place. So $G'$ must be acyclic.
\end{proof}
\end{Eg}
\end{defmark}

rows.tex_block:
\begin{defmark}
% claim_3_33
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{DefThm}[(Asymmetric) separoid axioms for $i\sigma$-separation/$id$-separation]
    \label{d-sep-separoid-axioms}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $A,B,C,D \ins J \cup V$ subsets of nodes.
Then the ternary relations $\iPerp = \idPerp_G$ and $\iPerp = \isPerp_G$ satisfy the following rules:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\alph*)]
    \item Extended Left Redundancy: 
    \item[] $D \ins A \implies D \iPerp  B \given A$.
    \item $J$-Restricted Right Redundancy: 
    \item[] $A \iPerp  \emptyset \given C \cup J$ always holds.
    \item $J$-Inverted Right Decomposition: 
    \item[] $A \iPerp  B \given C \implies A \iPerp  J \cup B \given C$.
	\item Left Decomposition:  
	\item[]$A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C \implies D \iPerp  B \given C$.
  \item Right Decomposition:  	
    \item[] $A \iPerp  B \cup D\given C \implies A \iPerp  D \given C$.
    \item Left Weak Union:
    \item[] $A \cup D \iPerp  B  \given C \implies A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C$.
  \item Right Weak Union:\item[] $A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C \implies A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C$.
\item Left Contraction:\item[] 
 $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (D \iPerp  B \given C) \implies A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C$.
\item Right Contraction:
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (A \iPerp  D \given C)  \implies A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C$.
\item Right Cross Contraction:
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (D \iPerp  A \given C) \implies A  \iPerp  B \cup D \given C$.
\item Flipped Left Cross Contraction: 
\item[] $ (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \land (B \iPerp  D \given C) \implies B \iPerp  A \cup D \given C$.
\end{enumerate}
In particular, we have the equivalences:
$$ (A \iPerp  B \cup D \given C) \quad\iff\quad (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \quad\land\quad (A \iPerp  D \given C)  ,$$
%and:
$$ (A \cup D \iPerp  B \given C) \quad\iff\quad (A \iPerp  B \given D \cup C) \quad\land\quad (D \iPerp  B \given C).$$
%Finally, if $J = \emptyset$ then 
We also get:
\begin{enumerate}[resume,label=\alph*)]
    \item $J$-Restricted Symmetry: 
	\item[] $A \iPerp  B \given C \cup J \implies B \iPerp A \given C \cup J$. 
\end{enumerate}
For the special case of $J=\emptyset$ we have thus (unrestricted) Symmetry.
\end{DefThm}
\end{claimmark}
\end{defmark}
